group.title: Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs
group.brief: Compile a C# validation rule once and run it on both the server and the browser.

body.p: [b]One rule, two runtimes, no drift.[/b]

Form validation almost always has to exist twice: once on the server, where it is authoritative and cannot be bypassed, and once in the browser, where it gives the user instant feedback without a round trip. Written by hand, those two copies drift apart - a rule tightened on the server is forgotten on the client, an edge case fixed in JavaScript never makes it back into C#, and the two ends quietly disagree about what "valid" means.

[b]Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs[/b] removes the second copy. You write the rule once, as an ordinary C# lambda - typically a boolean predicate over your model. The server runs that lambda directly, compiled to a normal delegate. The same lambda is translated into an equivalent JavaScript expression that runs in the browser. There is a single source of truth, so the client and server cannot disagree: change the C# predicate and both ends change together.

Because the emitted code is a faithful translation - same operators, same string and date handling, same null and numeric semantics - a value that passes (or fails) on one side passes (or fails) the same way on the other.
[b]The API at a glance[/b]
Type
Purpose
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]ExpressionCompiler[/clink]
The entry point: wrap a lambda, read the emitted JavaScript.
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions]Functions[/clink]
Validation helpers (age, Luhn check, lenient coercions, null tests) safe to call inside a rule, because each one has a matching JavaScript implementation.
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionToJsStubAccessor]ExpressionToJsStubAccessor[/clink]
The JavaScript runtime the emitted expressions depend on; load it into the page once.
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode[/clink]
Selects how dates are framed (local time or UTC) so both ends agree on the calendar.
This page covers [i]why[/i] the library exists. The guides listed below cover [i]how[/i] to use it.

group.title: Namespace Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs
group.brief: The common namespace for all of the library's classes.

body.p: [b]Entry points[/b]
Type
Purpose
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]ExpressionCompiler[/clink]
The entry point. Wrap a lambda, optionally configure it, then read [c]JavaScriptExpression[/c] to get the emitted code.
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionToJsStubAccessor]ExpressionToJsStubAccessor[/clink]
Hands you the JavaScript runtime ([c]stub.js[/c]) that the emitted code calls into. Load it into the page once.
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions]Functions[/clink]
Validation helpers (age math, Luhn check, lenient coercions, null tests) safe to call inside a rule, because each one has a matching JavaScript implementation.
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode[/clink]
Selects how dates are framed in the emitted code so both ends agree on the calendar.
[b]Extending[/b]

The compiler can be taught to translate methods, properties and constants on types the built-ins do not cover. For when and how, see [link=guides.extending]Extending the translator for your own types[/link].

article.title: Validating on all tiers from one source of truth
article.brief: Write a validation rule once in C#, run it on the server, and run the same rule in the browser.

body.p: The goal of this library is one rule, two runtimes. You write a validation predicate once, as a C# lambda. The server runs it directly as a compiled delegate; the browser runs an equivalent JavaScript expression emitted from the same lambda. Because both ends come from a single definition, they cannot drift apart.

flowchart TD R["C# rule, written once"] R --> C["Compile to a delegate"] R --> K["ExpressionCompiler"] C --> SV["Server: run the delegate"] K --> JS["JavaScript expression"] JS --> EV["Browser: eval with stub.js"] SV --> V["Same boolean verdict"] EV --> V
This walkthrough takes one rule from C# all the way into the page.
Step 1 - write the rule once
A rule is an ordinary [c]Expression<Func<...>>[/c], usually a boolean predicate over your model. Keep it an expression (not a statement lambda) so the compiler can walk it.
Step 2 - run it on the server
On the server the rule is just a delegate. Compile it and invoke it like any [c]Func[/c]. This is the authoritative check that cannot be bypassed.
bool ok = rule.Compile()(42); // true
Step 3 - emit the JavaScript

Hand the same lambda to [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]ExpressionCompiler[/clink] and read [c]JavaScriptExpression[/c]. The result is computed once and cached, so configure the compiler (date mode, custom translators) [i]before[/i] reading it.
string js = new ExpressionCompiler(rule).JavaScriptExpression; // js == "((jsv_greater(x, 0)) && (jsv_less(x, 100)))"

The emitted text is an [i]expression[/i], not a program. It calls small runtime helpers named [c]jsv_*[/c] ([c]jsv_greater[/c], [c]jsv_equal[/c], [c]jsv_match[/c], ...). Those helpers exist so JavaScript reproduces C# semantics - integer division, banker's rounding, typed equality, date handling - rather than JavaScript's looser defaults. See [link=guides.supported-features]Supported expression features[/link] for the full set.
Step 4 - ship the runtime stub to the page

The [c]jsv_*[/c] helpers live in [c]stub.js[/c], shipped inside the assembly as an embedded resource. Retrieve it with [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionToJsStubAccessor]ExpressionToJsStubAccessor.GetJsIncludesAsString[/clink] and include it on the page [b]once[/b], before evaluating any compiled expression. It is the same for every expression, so cache it site-wide rather than per rule.
string stub = ExpressionToJsStubAccessor.GetJsIncludesAsString();
There are two common ways to get that string to the browser.
[b]Render it inline[/b] - write the stub straight into the page, for example from a Razor layout so every page that validates has it:

example.title: Inline in an ASP.NET Razor layout

body.p: @* _Layout.cshtml - emit the runtime once, e.g. in the page head *@ <script> @Html.Raw(ExpressionToJsStubAccessor.GetJsIncludesAsString()) </script>

[b]Serve it as a static script[/b] - the stub is just a set of [c]jsv_*[/c] function definitions, so you can expose it at a stable URL and reference it with a normal [c]<script>[/c] tag (no wrapping needed - it defines the helpers as globals). It never changes between requests, so the browser caches it like any other static script - the better option for a busy site:

example.title: A REST endpoint that returns the stub

body.p: <!-- in the page head, before any emitted rule runs --> <script src="/js/expr-stub.js"></script>
Step 5 - evaluate in the browser
If you rendered the rule inline, bind the variables the expression references (here [c]x[/c]) and evaluate the emitted string. It returns the same boolean the server computed:

In a script-tag setup you fetch each rule from the server as well. A bare expression is not callable on its own, so wrap it in a [b]named function[/b] - the name fixed, or taken from the request - and return that:

example.title: A REST endpoint that returns the rule as a function

body.p: Load the stub once, the rule script alongside it, and call the function like any other:

example.title: Loading the rule and calling it

body.p: <!-- the jsv_* runtime, once --> <script src="/js/expr-stub.js"></script> <!-- defines function ageOk(x) --> <script src="/js/rules/ageOk"></script> <script> if (!ageOk(document.getElementById("age").value)) showError("Enter a value between 1 and 99."); </script>
Beyond a single value

The rule above validates one base-type value bound to a bare variable. Some rules instead have to see [b]several fields at once[/b] - for example, that a password and its confirmation match. Those are written against the whole model and bound to the form on the page, which is a little extra setup covered in its own guide: [link=guides.model-rules]Validating against a model object[/link].
See also
[link=guides.model-rules]Validating against a model object[/link]
[link=guides.supported-features]Supported expression features[/link]
[link=guides.unit-testing]Unit-testing your expressions[/link]

article.title: Validating against a model object
article.brief: Write rules that span several fields of a model, bound to the form on the page.

body.p: Most field checks need only that one field's value - a length, a digit, a postal-code format - and a single-value rule (the kind in the [link=guides.validation-walkthrough]walkthrough[/link]) handles them. A [b]model[/b] rule earns its place when a check has to see [b]more than one field at once[/b].

The classic example is a change-password form. Every rule is single-field - minimum length, contains a digit, and so on - except one: the new password and its confirmation must [i]match[/i]. That check cannot be expressed against a single value; it has to read both fields, so it is written against the whole model.

example.title: A rule that spans two fields

body.p: // the new password and its confirmation must be equal Expression<Func<Account, bool>> rule = m => m.Password == m.Confirm;
The problem: where will JavaScript get the object and its properties from?

By default the compiler emits each member access [i]verbatim[/i]: the rule above would become [c]jsv_equal(m.Password, m.Confirm)[/c]. That only works if the browser already has a JavaScript object [c]m[/c] shaped exactly like the server model. On a form you do not - the values live in input fields, not in a mirror of your C# model.
What you want instead is for each access to resolve against the [b]form[/b]: read [c]Password[/c] and [c]Confirm[/c] from the page.
The binding: reference()

The convention the library implements for you is a single host-provided JavaScript function, [c]reference()[/c]: by default a model access resolves to a [c]reference(...)[/c] call that reads the matching value from the page.
Hook
Provided by the page
[c]reference('Path.To.Field')[/c]
Returns the current value of a model field, given its path. Your page implements it to read the matching input (or a data model bound to the form).
With that binding the password-match rule emits against the form instead of a server-shaped object:
// rule: m => m.Password == m.Confirm // emits: jsv_equal(reference('Password'), reference('Confirm'))
A field reached through a longer chain becomes a dotted path the same way - [c]m.Address.PostalCode[/c] becomes [c]reference('Address.PostalCode')[/c].
Map the model to the form, once for the whole application

You are not binding a particular model - you are declaring, once for the whole application, [b]how any rule's model maps onto the client-side source[/b] (the form). The convention is the [c]reference()[/c] hook: a model field resolves to the matching value on the page.

This is [b]off by default[/b]: out of the box the compiler emits member access verbatim. You turn the [c]reference()[/c] convention on with [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsParameterRegistry]Parameters.MapReference[/clink], whose built-in rendering makes the bare parameter [c]reference()[/c], a member chain [c]reference('Path.To.Field')[/c], and array indexing [c]jsv_index(...)[/c] - so [c]m.Password[/c], in any rule over any model, becomes [c]reference('Password')[/c].

[c]MapReference[/c] takes a [c]Func<Type, bool>[/c] that picks which parameters it applies to; passing [c]_ => true[/c] enables it for [b]every parameter, of any type, in any rule[/b]. (You could filter by type, but a validation rule's one parameter is always the model, so there is nothing to filter.) It only ever touches the rule's own parameter, so a parameter introduced by a nested LINQ lambda ([c]r => r.Length > 0[/c]) keeps its own name.
Because it is one unified convention, enable it [b]once[/b] in a small application-level factory and run every rule through it - no rule repeats the setup:

example.title: Compile every rule through one factory

body.p: The host side

The page provides the hook the emitted code calls: [c]reference(path)[/c], which walks the form by a dotted path and returns a field's current value. Write [c]reference[/c] once for your app, alongside the [link=guides.validation-walkthrough]runtime stub[/link].
Serve each rule wrapped as a function, compiled through the factory above. Because the rule reads its inputs through [c]reference()[/c], the function takes no arguments:
On the page, implement [c]reference[/c] against your form and call the rule:

example.title: Host page - reference() and running the rule

body.p:
<form name="account">
    <input name="Password" type="password">
    <input name="Confirm"  type="password">
</form>
 
<!-- jsv_* runtime, then the rule (defines passwordsMatch()) -->
<script src="/js/expr-stub.js"></script>
<script src="/js/rules/passwordsMatch"></script>
<script>
    // written once for the app: resolve a model path against the form
    function reference(path) {
        return document.forms["account"].elements[path].value;
    }
 
    document.forms["account"].addEventListener("submit", function (e) {
        if (!passwordsMatch()) {
            e.preventDefault();
            showError("Passwords do not match.");
        }
    });
</script>
Referring to the model another way

[c]MapReference[/c] emits [c]reference('path')[/c], which is all most pages need. When it isn't enough, [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsParameterRegistry]Map[/clink] lets you emit anything - and it takes [b]two[/b] functions, because a rule can use the model in two shapes:
the [b]whole model[/b] (the bare parameter) - for example when you pass it to a custom whole-entity check; and
a [b]single field[/b] (a member chain rooted in the parameter), such as [c]m.Password[/c].
A custom whole-entity function shows both at once - register the function (server + client), then a rule that uses the model whole [i]and[/i] by field:

The first [c]Map[/c] function renders the bare parameter (the whole model); the second renders a member or index chain rooted in it. [c]MapReference[/c] simply fills both in for you ([c]reference()[/c] and [c]reference('path')[/c]).
More than one form on the page

With several forms on one page, [c]reference('Password')[/c] is ambiguous. Pass the model's type as the form name so the page knows which form to read. The type is on the [c]ParameterExpression[/c], and [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]ParameterAccessPath[/clink] turns the chain into the dotted path:
On the page, the matching [c]reference[/c] reads the named form:
An object instead of a lookup function

To skip the lookup function, refer to the model as an object the page exposes: the whole model is [c]value[/c] and a field is [c]value.Password[/c]. Nothing is replaced by the bare word [c]value[/c] - it [i]is[/i] the object the fields hang off:
Whatever you emit, the only contract is that the [link=guides.client-frameworks]page provides[/link] whatever the emitted code references.
See also
[link=guides.validation-walkthrough]Validating on all tiers from one source of truth[/link]
[link=guides.client-frameworks]Wiring into client frameworks[/link]
[link=guides.extending]Extending the translator for your own types[/link]

article.title: Wiring into client frameworks
article.brief: Attach a compiled rule to the validation lifecycle of ASP.NET, jQuery, Angular, React or Vue.

body.p: By the time a rule reaches the page, the library has done its part: you have the [link=guides.validation-walkthrough]runtime stub[/link] and a compiled rule. Wrapped as a function (see the walkthrough and [link=guides.model-rules]model rules[/link]), that rule is just a [b]boolean[/b]:
a [b]single-field[/b] rule takes the field value - [c]ageOk(value)[/c]; or
a [b]whole-model[/b] rule reads the model - [c]passwordsMatch()[/c] via [c]reference()[/c], or [c]rule(model)[/c] when you compile it for object access.

Wiring it into any framework is therefore the same two steps: [b]call the boolean at the right moment, and map [c]false[/c] to an error message[/b]. What differs per framework is only [i]where[/i] that moment is and how errors are surfaced.

The named rule function ([c]ageOk[/c], [c]passwordsMatch[/c]) gets onto the page one of two ways, both from the [link=guides.validation-walkthrough]walkthrough[/link]: a server-rendered page [b]embeds[/b] it inline (the server already holds the compiler), while a single-page app [b]serves[/b] it as a script or bundles it. The ASP.NET section embeds; the rest assume the function is already loaded.
Where does the rule read its inputs?

One choice decides the rest. A [b]DOM-based[/b] stack (server-rendered pages, jQuery) keeps the values in form inputs, so a whole-model rule's [c]reference()[/c] can read them straight from the DOM. A [b]component[/b] stack (Angular, React, Vue) keeps state in the component, not the DOM - so there, compile whole-model rules for [link=guides.model-rules]object access[/link] ([c]value.Field[/c]) and call [c]rule(model)[/c] with the component's own state. Single-field rules are the same everywhere: hand the field value to [c]rule(value)[/c].
ASP.NET

The server is already rendering the page and already holds the compiler, so [b]embed[/b] the rule inline rather than fetching it separately: emit it as a function with Razor, right where it is used (the stub goes in the layout, as in the [link=guides.validation-walkthrough]walkthrough[/link]). Then call it from a [c]CustomValidator[/c]'s client function - [c]args.Value[/c] is the field, [c]args.IsValid[/c] is the verdict:

example.title: Embedding the rule and calling it

body.p: For a whole-model rule, emit it the same way (compiled for [c]reference()[/c], as a no-argument function), attach the validator to any field, and call it - [c]reference()[/c] reads the rest of the form.
jQuery
Register the rule as a custom method, then name it in the field's rules. [c]this.optional(element)[/c] keeps an empty field from failing (let a [c]required[/c] rule own that):
A whole-model rule (such as the password match) is a method on the confirmation field that ignores its argument and calls the no-argument rule.
Angular

In reactive forms a validator is a function returning [c]null[/c] when valid or an error object when not. Put a single-field rule on the control; put a whole-model rule on the [c]FormGroup[/c] so it sees every field:
React
React has no built-in validation, so call the rule wherever you check the form - here a controlled component derives validity on each render:

With a form library the rule drops into its hook - for react-hook-form, [c]register("age", { validate: v => ageOk(v) || "Enter a value between 1 and 99." })[/c]. A whole-model rule (compiled for object access) takes the form's values object: [c]rule(getValues())[/c].
Vue
In Vue derive validity from state with a computed value:
With VeeValidate, the rule becomes a custom rule: [c]defineRule("ageOk", ageOk)[/c].
See also
[link=guides.validation-walkthrough]Validating on all tiers from one source of truth[/link]
[link=guides.model-rules]Validating against a model object[/link]

article.title: Supported expression features
article.brief: The C# operators, methods and constructs you can use in a rule, and the ones you cannot.

body.p: A rule is an expression tree, and the compiler translates the constructs listed here. Anything not listed throws at compile time (when you read [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]JavaScriptExpression[/clink]), so an unsupported construct fails loudly on the server rather than silently misbehaving in the browser.

Two principles run through everything below. First, [b]C# semantics win[/b]: the emitted [c]jsv_*[/c] helpers reproduce C# behaviour, not JavaScript's - integer division truncates toward zero, [c]int[/c] arithmetic wraps on overflow, [c]Math.Round[/c] uses banker's rounding, and [c]==[/c] is typed and strict (no [c]"10" == 10[/c]); see [link=guides.unit-testing]Unit-testing your expressions[/link]. Second, [b]constant subtrees are folded[/b]: a subtree with no free parameter and no ambient [c]Now[/c]/[c]Today[/c]/[c]UtcNow[/c] is evaluated at compile time and emitted as a literal.
Operators
Category
Supported
Arithmetic
[c]+[/c] [c]-[/c] [c]*[/c] [c]/[/c] [c]%[/c], unary [c]-[/c], and power. Integer division truncates and [c]int[/c] results wrap, matching C#.
Comparison
[c]<[/c] [c]<=[/c] [c]>[/c] [c]>=[/c] [c]==[/c] [c]!=[/c]. Equality is strict and typed; two numbers compare with a small tolerance so [c]0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3[/c] holds.
Logical
[c]&&[/c] [c]||[/c] [c]![/c], and logical XOR ([c]^[/c] on [c]bool[/c]).
Bitwise
[c]&[/c] [c]|[/c] [c]^[/c] on integers (wrapped to 32-bit).
Other
Conditional [c]?:[/c], null-coalescing [c]??[/c], array indexing [c]a[[nil]i][/c], and array/collection length.
Methods, members and helpers
Area
Supported
Math ([c]System.Math[/c])

[c]Round[/c] (banker's, including the [c]Round(x, digits)[/c] overload), [c]Floor[/c], [c]Ceiling[/c], [c]Truncate[/c], [c]Abs[/c], [c]Sign[/c], [c]Sqrt[/c], [c]Pow[/c], [c]Log[/c], [c]Exp[/c], [c]Min[/c], [c]Max[/c], the trigonometric functions, and [c]Math.PI[/c].
Strings

[c]Trim[/c]/[c]TrimStart[/c]/[c]TrimEnd[/c], [c]ToUpper[/c]/[c]ToLower[/c] (and the [c]Invariant[/c] variants), [c]StartsWith[/c]/[c]EndsWith[/c] (including the [c]StringComparison[/c] overload), [c]Contains[/c], [c]IndexOf[/c]/[c]LastIndexOf[/c], [c]Replace[/c], [c]PadLeft[/c]/[c]PadRight[/c] (with optional pad char), [c]Length[/c], the indexer, and [c]ToString[/c].
Static string tests
[c]string.IsNullOrEmpty[/c], [c]string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace[/c] (true Unicode whitespace, matching C#).
Characters
[c]char.IsUpper[/c], [c]IsLower[/c], [c]IsDigit[/c], [c]IsLetter[/c], [c]IsLetterOrDigit[/c], [c]IsPunctuation[/c], [c]IsControl[/c], [c]IsWhiteSpace[/c].
Regex
[c]Regex.IsMatch(input, pattern)[/c]. The pattern is escaped into a JS regex literal.
Collections (LINQ)

[c]Any[/c], [c]All[/c], [c]Count[/c], [c]First[/c], [c]Last[/c], [c]FirstOrDefault[/c]/[c]LastOrDefault[/c] (with a predicate), [c]Where[/c], [c]Select[/c], [c]Sum[/c], [c]Min[/c], [c]Max[/c], [c]Distinct[/c], [c]Contains[/c]. They return real JS values, so they chain.
Dates and times

Component reads [c]Year[/c]/[c]Month[/c]/[c]Day[/c]/[c]Hour[/c]/[c]Minute[/c]/[c]Second[/c]/[c]DayOfWeek[/c] (framed by [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode[/clink]); [c]AddDays[/c]/[c]AddHours[/c]/[c]AddMinutes[/c]/[c]AddSeconds[/c]; adding/subtracting a [c]TimeSpan[/c]; subtracting two dates; the [c]TimeSpan.Total*[/c] properties; and the dynamic clock [c]Now[/c]/[c]Today[/c]/[c]UtcNow[/c]. See [link=guides.dates]Dates and time zones[/link].
Parity helpers ([clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions]Functions[/clink])

Checks with no direct BCL-to-JS equivalent, shipped as matched pairs (a [c]Functions[/c] method and an identical [c]jsv_*[/c] twin): [c]DaysSince[/c], [c]MonthsSince[/c], [c]YearsSince[/c], [c]IsCreditCardNumberCorrect[/c] (Luhn), [c]ToBool[/c], [c]ToInt[/c], [c]Fractional[/c], [c]IsNull[/c], [c]IsNotNull[/c], [c]IsNullOrEmpty[/c], [c]IsNotNullOrEmpty[/c].
Enums

Emitted as the underlying numeric value (flags enums combine), so client-side enum fields must carry the numeric value. To emit an enum by [i]name[/i] instead, register a constant translator - see [link=guides.extending]Extending the translator for your own types[/link].
Not supported
These throw at compile time, so they fail loudly on the server rather than misbehaving in the browser:
Construct
Why / alternative
String [c]+[/c] concat, [c]string.Format[/c], [c]string.Split[/c]
Locale and format correctness on the JS side is out of scope. ([c]+[/c] on strings compiles to a [c]string.Concat[/c] call, not an [c]Add[/c].)
[c]DateTime.AddMonths[/c]/[c]AddYears[/c], [c]DateTime.Parse[/c], [c]TimeSpan.FromX[/c], [c]DateTimeOffset[/c]
Not translated; use the supported date arithmetic, or a custom translator.
Bit shifts [c]<<[/c] [c]>>[/c], [c]int.TryParse[/c]/[c]double.TryParse[/c], LINQ [c]Average[/c], predicate-less [c]FirstOrDefault[/c]/[c]LastOrDefault[/c]
Not yet translated.
[c]long[/c] beyond 2^53
Loses precision in JavaScript and is not made safe.
Statement-bodied lambdas
Use a single expression.

The not-supported list reflects the current built-ins; most gaps can be closed for your own code with a custom translator - see [link=guides.extending]Extending the translator for your own types[/link].
See also
[link=guides.dates]Dates and time zones[/link]
[link=guides.extending]Extending the translator for your own types[/link]
[link=guides.unit-testing]Unit-testing your expressions[/link]

article.title: Dates and time zones
article.brief: Pick a date frame with DateTimeMode so the server and the browser agree on the calendar.

body.p: JavaScript [c]Date[/c] objects carry a time zone; C# [c]DateTime[/c] values are ambiguous about one. To keep the two ends in agreement, the compiler emits every [i]calendar[/i] construct in a frame you choose: [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode[/clink].
Choosing the frame
Mode
Emits
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode.Local[/clink] (default)
Local-time constructs - [c]new Date(y, m, d, ...)[/c] and [c]getFullYear()[/c] / [c]getMonth()[/c] / ...
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode.Utc[/clink]
UTC constructs - [c]new Date(Date.UTC(y, m, d, ...))[/c] and [c]getUTCFullYear()[/c] / [c]getUTCMonth()[/c] / ...

Set the mode through the [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]DateMode[/clink] property or the [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]ExpressionCompiler(lambda, mode)[/clink] constructor. It must be set [b]before[/b] you read [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]JavaScriptExpression[/clink], because the result is computed once and cached.
The contract

Bind JS-side dates in the [b]same[/b] frame you select here. A mismatch does not error - it silently shifts every calendar-component comparison by the client's UTC offset, so a rule can quietly pass or fail by up to a day.

The mode only governs constructs that read or build [i]calendar components[/i]: date constants, the [c]Year[/c]/[c]Month[/c]/[c]Day[/c]/[c]Hour[/c]/... reads, and the calendar-based [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions]MonthsSince[/clink] / [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions]YearsSince[/clink] helpers (which receive a UTC flag).
What is frame-independent
Operations that work on the absolute instant (the epoch millisecond) are the same in both frames, so you never have to think about the mode for them:
Adding or subtracting a [c]TimeSpan[/c], and subtracting two dates.
[c]AddDays[/c] / [c]AddHours[/c] / [c]AddMinutes[/c] / [c]AddSeconds[/c].
The [c]TimeSpan.Total*[/c] properties and [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions]DaysSince[/clink].
The clock stays live

[c]DateTime.Now[/c], [c]DateTime.Today[/c] and [c]DateTime.UtcNow[/c] are [i]not[/i] folded into a compile-time constant. They emit live JavaScript - [c]Now[/c]/[c]UtcNow[/c] become [c]new Date()[/c], and [c]Today[/c] becomes [c]jsv_today(...)[/c] (mode-aware midnight) - so the rule is evaluated against the clock when the user submits, exactly as the server would. Chains such as [c]DateTime.Now.AddDays(30)[/c] stay dynamic too.

example.title: An age rule, checked at validation time

body.p: // p => Functions.YearsSince(DateTime.Today, p.BirthDate) >= 21 // jsv_greaterorequal(jsv_yearssince(jsv_today(false), p.BirthDate, false), 21)
See also
[link=guides.supported-features]Supported expression features[/link]

article.title: Extending the translator for your own types
article.brief: Map a domain method, constant type or property the built-ins do not cover, without subclassing.

body.p: Real models reference types the built-ins don't know: a domain helper, a value struct, a [c]Guid[/c]. When a rule uses one, the compiler [b]throws at compile time[/b] rather than guess - so a gap surfaces on the server, not as wrong JavaScript in the browser. You close the gap by teaching the compiler the mapping, through one of three registries on the compiler instance.

classDiagram ExpressionCompiler --> IJsMethodRegistry : Methods ExpressionCompiler --> IJsConstantRegistry : Constants ExpressionCompiler --> IJsMemberRegistry : Members IJsMethodRegistry ..> IMethodCallTranslator IJsConstantRegistry ..> IConstantTranslator IJsMemberRegistry ..> IMemberTranslator IMethodCallTranslator ..> IExpressionEmitContext IConstantTranslator ..> IExpressionEmitContext IMemberTranslator ..> IExpressionEmitContext

Register [b]before[/b] reading [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]JavaScriptExpression[/clink]; registrations are consulted ahead of the built-ins, so they can also deliberately [i]shadow[/i] a default. The three scenarios below use one order-form model:

Only constructs that read a parameter ([c]o.something[/c]) reach the client; a subtree with no parameter is computed on the server and emitted as a literal. So these scenarios are all about [b]a model value the browser has to work with[/b].
Add a method for a type
[b]When:[/b] your rule calls a domain helper on a model value - a server-side check with no JavaScript twin, such as [c]Coupon.IsValid(o.CouponCode)[/c]:

Map it to a JavaScript function with [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsMethodRegistry]MapMethod[/clink]; in the template, [c]$0[/c], [c]$1[/c], ... are the call's arguments (and [c]$obj[/c] the instance, for an instance method):

example.title: Map the call to a JS function

body.p: [b]The JavaScript side:[/b] the emitted code calls [c]jsv_coupon_ok[/c], so define it on the page - the same check in JavaScript, next to the [link=guides.validation-walkthrough]runtime stub[/link]:
Add a property read for a type

[b]When:[/b] a rule reads a property the built-ins don't translate, so the value must be computed on the client. [c]DateTime.DayOfYear[/c] is a good example: a JavaScript [c]Date[/c] has no such property, yet by default the compiler emits the access verbatim - silently wrong:

example.title: The default - verbatim, and wrong for a JS Date

body.p: // o => o.PlacedOn.DayOfYear <= 31 // -> jsv_lessorequal(o.PlacedOn.DayOfYear, 31) // a JS Date has no .DayOfYear
Map the property to a real client computation with [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsMemberRegistry]MapMember[/clink]; [c]$obj[/c] is the emitted target the property is read from:

example.title: Map the property to a helper

body.p: [b]The JavaScript side:[/b] ship the [c]jsv_dayofyear[/c] helper that computes it from a [c]Date[/c]:
Map an enum to the client

[b]When:[/b] the client carries an enum by [i]name[/i] rather than by number. By default an enum constant emits its underlying numeric value (so [c]OrderStatus.Shipped[/c] becomes [c]1[/c]); [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsConstantRegistry]MapConstant[/clink] maps the enum [b]type[/b] to a different literal - here its name:

[b]The JavaScript side:[/b] a data contract - the client must carry [c]Status[/c] as the matching string. One caveat: this fires where the enum stays enum-typed (a method argument, as above). A direct [c]o.Status == OrderStatus.Shipped[/c] is lifted to an [c]int[/c] comparison by the C# compiler, so the constant is already a number and the map does not apply there.
When a template is not enough

A template is a fixed string with [c]$0[/c]/[c]$obj[/c] holes. When the emitted form has to vary - on argument types, arity, or the [i]value[/i] of an argument - implement the matching interface instead and register it with [c]AddTranslator[/c]: [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IMethodCallTranslator]IMethodCallTranslator[/clink], [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IConstantTranslator]IConstantTranslator[/clink] or [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IMemberTranslator]IMemberTranslator[/clink]. Each receives the node and an [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IExpressionEmitContext]IExpressionEmitContext[/clink] (to recurse into sub-expressions with [c]Emit[/c], and to escape strings/regex), and returns whether it handled the node. These are the very interfaces the built-ins are written against.

For example, suppose [c]Coupon.IsValid[/c] gains a [c]strict[/c] flag that should select a different JavaScript function - a choice a fixed template can't make. The translator reads the constant argument and emits accordingly:

example.title: A method translator that branches on an argument

body.p: Returning [c]false[/c] passes the node on, so a translator handles only the shapes it recognises.
Your mapping, your runtime

Whatever a template or translator emits must exist at evaluation time. If you emit [c]jsv_coupon_ok(...)[/c], defining [c]jsv_coupon_ok[/c] in the JavaScript on the page is your responsibility. The library's contract is narrow: it emits text and ships its own [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionToJsStubAccessor]stub[/clink] - there is deliberately no companion-JS registration API, so how you bundle your own helpers is up to you.
Parameter rendering is separate

These registries cover [i]types[/i]. How a rule's [i]parameter[/i] renders - the form-validation [c]reference('path')[/c] binding - is a different registry, [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsParameterRegistry]compiler.Parameters[/clink], covered in [link=guides.model-rules]Validating against a model object[/link].
See also
[link=guides.model-rules]Validating against a model object[/link]
[link=guides.supported-features]Supported expression features[/link]

article.title: Unit-testing your expressions
article.brief: Prove the C# rule and its emitted JavaScript agree, by running both and comparing - the way this library tests itself.

body.p: A validation rule is a [b]boolean[/b] - it answers valid or not. Parity means the C# delegate and the emitted JavaScript return the [b]same true/false[/b] for the same input. That is what a differential test checks: run both, compare the verdicts. It matters because C# and JavaScript diverge in ways that can [i]flip[/i] that verdict - integer division, banker's rounding, date framing, [c]==[/c] coercion, Unicode whitespace. The emitted [c]jsv_*[/c] helpers exist to keep the two ends agreeing; a test is how you confirm they do for [i]your[/i] rule.
The setup: an in-process JavaScript engine

You do not need a browser. Run the emitted JavaScript in-process with [eurl=https://github.com/sebastienros/jint]Jint[/eurl], a JavaScript engine for .NET. The one rule that makes it work: load the runtime stub ([clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionToJsStubAccessor]ExpressionToJsStubAccessor[/clink]) into the engine once, so the [c]jsv_*[/c] helpers the emitted code calls are defined.
Because the result is always a boolean, the assertion is simple - no numeric coercion, just [c]bool[/c] against [c]bool[/c]:

example.title: A reusable differential assertion for a rule (xUnit)

body.p: Binding the input
The verdict comparison is trivial; the care is in [b]binding the input[/b] the same way the browser will:

[b]Dates[/b] must be bound in the same frame as the compiler's [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode[/clink] - bind a UTC date for a [c]Utc[/c] rule, a local one for a [c]Local[/c] rule. A frame mismatch in the test is the same bug it would be in production; see [link=guides.dates]Dates and time zones[/link].

[b]Model rules[/b] need the parameter bound as an object Jint can read ([c]SetValue("p", model)[/c]). If you compiled the rule for the [link=guides.model-rules]form binding[/link] ([c]reference()[/c]), define a [c]reference[/c] function in the engine too, so the emitted lookups resolve.
What to test
Sampling round numbers hides exactly the bugs differential testing exists to catch. Aim your inputs at the places C# and JavaScript disagree:
Integer division and modulo with operands that do not divide evenly ([c]11 / 2[/c]).
[c]Math.Round[/c] on midpoints ([c]0.5[/c], [c]2.5[/c]) - banker's rounding.
Equality across types and the float-tolerance case ([c]0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3[/c]).
[c]null[/c] / empty inputs - strings, collections, nullable values.
Dates near midnight and across month/year boundaries, in the [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode[/clink] you ship.
How this library tests itself

The same pattern guards the library. Its [c]Complete/[/c] differential suite checks each case three ways - the compiled C# delegate, the emitted JavaScript in Jint, and the two against each other - and that comparison is what keeps every server-side [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions]Functions[/clink] helper in lock-step with its [c]jsv_*[/c] twin in the stub. Pointing the same harness at [i]your[/i] rules is the surest way to know your client and server agree.
See also
[link=guides.validation-walkthrough]Validating on all tiers from one source of truth[/link]
[link=guides.supported-features]Supported expression features[/link]
[link=guides.dates]Dates and time zones[/link]

class.name: DateTimeMode
class.brief: How DateTime values are framed in the emitted JavaScript.

member.name: Local
member.brief: Local time: new Date(y, m, d, ...) and getFullYear()/getMonth()/...

member.name: Utc
member.brief: UTC: new Date(Date.UTC(y, m, d, ...)) and getUTCFullYear()/getUTCMonth()/...

class.name: ExpressionCompiler
class.brief: Converts a C# lambda expression into an equivalent JavaScript expression string.

body.p: This is the entry point of the library. Construct it around a lambda - typically a boolean form-validation predicate - and read [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression.B2E]ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression[/clink] to get JavaScript that computes the same result in the browser. The point is to keep one source of truth: the lambda runs server-side as a compiled delegate, and its emitted twin runs client-side, so a rule cannot drift between the two ends.

Configure framing and translation [i]before[/i] reading the expression: set [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.DateMode.Ht5]ExpressionCompiler.DateMode[/clink] for date semantics, and register custom translations through [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.Methods.p1B]ExpressionCompiler.Methods[/clink] , [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.Constants.PQA]ExpressionCompiler.Constants[/clink] , and [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.Members.qX6]ExpressionCompiler.Members[/clink] . For deeper control - notably how a parameter renders on the client - derive a subclass and override the [c]protected virtual[/c] emit methods. Whatever the emitted code calls (the [c]jsv_*[/c] helpers) must be present at evaluation time; see [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionToJsStubAccessor.GetJsIncludesAsString.8V5]ExpressionToJsStubAccessor.GetJsIncludesAsString[/clink] .

member.name: ReturnType
member.brief: The CLR type the lambda produces.

body.p: Inspect it to decide how to consume the emitted code - most commonly to confirm a validation lambda yields a [c]bool[/c] before wiring its JavaScript into a form's validity check.

member.name: DateMode
member.brief: How DateTime values are framed in the emitted JavaScript - local time or UTC.

body.p: Selects [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode.Local.vt1]DateTimeMode.Local[/clink] (default) or [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode.Utc.yo6]DateTimeMode.Utc[/clink] . Set it before reading [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression.B2E]ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression[/clink] . The host must bind JS-side dates in the same frame (see the library docs / PLAN.md).

member.name: Methods
member.brief: Registry for mapping method calls the built-ins don't cover to JavaScript.

body.p: Register here when the lambda calls a method the built-ins don't handle (a domain helper, an overload with different arguments) so it maps to JavaScript instead of throwing at compile time. Register before reading [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression.B2E]ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression[/clink] ; your registrations are consulted ahead of the built-ins, which also lets you deliberately override one.

member.name: Constants
member.brief: Registry for mapping constant types the built-ins don't cover to JavaScript.

body.p: Register here to teach the compiler how to emit a constant of a type it doesn't already handle (a , a domain value struct) or to emit an enum by name rather than by its numeric value. Register before reading [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression.B2E]ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression[/clink] .

member.name: Members
member.brief: Registry for mapping property reads the built-ins don't cover to JavaScript.

body.p: Register here when the lambda reads a property the built-ins don't handle and you want it to map to a specific JavaScript form. Register before reading
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression.B2E]ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression[/clink] .

member.name: Parameters
member.brief: Registry for binding a parameter type to a host-side lookup instead of emitting it verbatim.

body.p: Register a model type here (with [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsParameterRegistry.MapReference.lsF]IJsParameterRegistry.MapReference[/clink] ) so rules over that type emit against the form through a [c]reference('path')[/c] hook rather than a server-shaped object - the form-validation case, with no subclassing. Register before reading [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression.B2E]ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression[/clink] .

member.name: JavaScriptExpression
member.brief: The emitted JavaScript - the whole reason to use this type.

body.p: Read it after the compiler is fully configured (date mode and any custom registrations applied); the result is computed on first access and cached, so later configuration changes are not reflected. Send the string to the client to evaluate, having first loaded the runtime stub ( [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionToJsStubAccessor.GetJsIncludesAsString.8V5]ExpressionToJsStubAccessor.GetJsIncludesAsString[/clink] ).

member.name: ExpressionCompiler
member.brief: Creates a compiler for the given lambda using local-time date framing.

body.p: Use this overload unless the client binds dates in UTC; pass [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode]DateTimeMode[/clink] explicitly (or set [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.DateMode.Ht5]ExpressionCompiler.DateMode[/clink] ) when it does.

The expression to translate - usually a validation predicate. Its parameters become the free variables of the emitted JavaScript, so they determine what the client code reads from; its body is what gets translated.

member.name: ExpressionCompiler
member.brief: Creates a compiler for the given lambda with an explicit date framing.

body.p: Use this when you already know whether the client binds dates in local time or UTC and want to fix it at construction.
The expression to translate. Its parameters become the free variables of the emitted JavaScript; its body is what gets translated.

Sets [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.DateMode.Ht5]ExpressionCompiler.DateMode[/clink] . Choose the frame the host will bind JS-side dates in: [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode.Local.vt1]DateTimeMode.Local[/clink] emits local-time constructs, [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode.Utc.yo6]DateTimeMode.Utc[/clink] emits UTC ones. A mismatch silently shifts every calendar-component comparison by the client's offset.

member.name: AddConstant
member.brief: Renders a single value as the JavaScript literal the compiler would emit for it.

body.p: Use it when you are building or testing JavaScript fragments by hand and need one constant formatted the same way the compiler does; it covers only the built-in types and does not apply any custom [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.Constants.PQA]ExpressionCompiler.Constants[/clink] registrations, so prefer a full [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]ExpressionCompiler[/clink] for real translation.
The value to render. Its runtime type selects the literal form (string, number, boolean, date, enum, ...); [c]null[/c] renders as [c]null[/c]. A type the built-ins don't recognize throws.

member.name: FormatDateLiteral
member.brief: Renders a fixed date as the JavaScript Date literal the compiler would emit for it.

body.p: Use it when hand-authoring a JavaScript fragment that must agree with compiled expressions on date framing - call it with the same [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.DateMode.Ht5]ExpressionCompiler.DateMode[/clink] you give the compiler so both sides build dates the same way.
The date to render. A midnight value emits a date-only constructor; any non-zero time component emits the longer form down to seconds. The value's is ignored - alone decides the frame.

Which frame to build the date in: [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode.Local.vt1]DateTimeMode.Local[/clink] for a local-time constructor, [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.DateTimeMode.Utc.yo6]DateTimeMode.Utc[/clink] for a UTC one. This must match how the rest of the page interprets dates or the literal will be off by the client's offset.

member.name: EscapeJsString
member.brief: Turns a CLR string into a safe, quoted JavaScript string literal.

body.p: Use it from a custom translator (or when assembling JavaScript by hand) whenever you splice user- or data-supplied text into emitted code, so quotes, backslashes, and control characters can't break or inject into the output.
The text to embed. Its characters are escaped as needed; the result already includes the surrounding quotes, so do not add your own.

member.name: EscapeJsRegex
member.brief: Prepares a regular-expression pattern for use inside a JavaScript regex literal.

body.p: Use it from a custom translator that emits a regex literal so an unescaped [c]/[/c] or a raw newline in the pattern doesn't terminate or corrupt the literal.

The regex source. Only the characters that would break a slash-delimited literal are adjusted; existing backslash escapes are respected and left intact. The result is the inner pattern text only - wrap it in the slashes (and any flags) yourself.

member.name: ParameterAccessPath
member.brief: Builds the dotted member path of a parameter access, for use inside a custom parameter

body.p: binding.

Turns a member chain rooted in a rule parameter into a path - [c]m.Address.PostalCode[/c] becomes [c]Address.PostalCode[/c], and the bare parameter becomes an empty string. Call it from the [c]parameterAccess[/c] function you pass to
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsParameterRegistry.Map.5EE]IJsParameterRegistry.Map[/clink] so you don't have to walk the expression yourself.
The member chain rooted in the parameter (as handed to your function).

member.name: Equals
member.brief: Tells whether two compilers were built from the same lambda.

body.p: Useful as a cache key when you memoize emitted JavaScript and want to avoid recompiling the same rule. Equality is by the underlying expression, not by the emitted string or configuration.
The compiler to compare against; [c]null[/c] is never equal.

member.name: Equals
member.brief: Tells whether this compiler was built from the given lambda.

body.p: The same caching/identity use as the other overload, for when what you hold is the raw expression rather than a compiler.
The expression to compare against this compiler's lambda; [c]null[/c] is never equal.

class.name: ExpressionToJsStubAccessor
class.brief: Gives you the JavaScript runtime that every compiled expression depends on.

body.p: The strings produced by [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression.B2E]ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression[/clink] call into a set of [c]jsv_*[/c] helper functions (for null-safe arithmetic, string handling, date math, LINQ, and so on). Those helpers live in this stub, which is embedded in the assembly. Emit the stub into the page (or otherwise load it into the JS engine) [i]once[/i], before any compiled expression is evaluated; the emitted expressions are useless without it. Typical use is to drop the returned text inside a [c]<script>[/c] block in your page layout.

member.name: GetJsIncludesAsString
member.brief: Returns the full jsv_* JavaScript runtime stub to embed in your page.

body.p: Call it once during page composition and place the returned text inside a script block so the browser has it loaded before any compiled expression runs. The content is fixed and cached after the first call, so repeated calls are cheap and you may call it freely from view code.

class.name: Functions
class.brief: Validation helpers you call from inside the lambdas you compile to JavaScript.

body.p: Reach for these instead of an arbitrary BCL call whenever the operation you need has no direct, translatable JavaScript form (a Luhn check, a calendar-aware "months since", a lenient string-to-bool coercion, ...). Every method here is guaranteed to have a matching [c]jsv_*[/c] implementation in the embedded runtime stub, so an expression that uses them compiles to JavaScript [i]and[/i] runs unchanged as a server-side delegate - the single source of truth the library exists to provide. A plain BCL method with no such mapping throws at compile time, so prefer the helper here when one fits.

member.name: Fractional
member.brief: Gets the part of a number after the decimal point, for whole-number or decimal-place checks.

body.p: Use it to reject values that must be whole ([c]Functions.Fractional(x) == 0[/c]) or to validate that a money amount has at most two decimal places.
The number to inspect. The sign is ignored: the magnitude is taken first, so [c]-3.25[/c] yields [c]0.25[/c] exactly as [c]3.25[/c] does. An integral value yields [c]0[/c].

member.name: DaysSince
member.brief: Counts whole calendar days between two dates, ignoring the time of day.

body.p: Use it for "within N days" rules - expiry windows, cool-down periods, recent-activity checks - where only the calendar day matters and the time of day must not skew the count.
The reference (usually later) date; its time component is discarded.

The baseline (usually earlier) date; its time component is discarded. The argument order sets the sign of the result: [c]args0[/c] after [c]args1[/c] gives a positive count, before it gives a negative count.

member.name: MonthsSince
member.brief: Counts completed calendar months between two dates, ignoring partial months.

body.p: Use it for age-in-months or tenure rules where a partial month should not count - the result advances only when a full calendar month has elapsed, not on a raw 30-day division.
The reference (usually later) date; its time component is discarded.

The baseline (usually earlier) date; its time component is discarded. As with [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions.DaysSince.pc1]Functions.DaysSince[/clink] , the argument order sets the sign of the result.

member.name: YearsSince
member.brief: Counts completed calendar years between two dates - the usual way to check age.

body.p: Use it for the canonical "must be at least N years old" rule ([c]Functions.YearsSince(DateTime.Today, birthDate) >= 18[/c]): the count advances only on a completed calendar year, so it matches how age is reckoned in practice.
The reference (usually later) date, e.g. today; its time component is discarded.
The baseline (usually earlier) date, e.g. a birth date; its time component is discarded. The argument order sets the sign of the result.

member.name: IsCreditCardNumberCorrect
member.brief: Validates a payment card number with the Luhn checksum, client and server alike.

body.p: Use it to catch typos in a typed-in card number before submission. It confirms the number passes the Luhn check only; it does not verify the card exists or is active.

The card number as entered. Embedded spaces and dashes are tolerated (so grouped input like [c]"4111 1111 1111 1111"[/c] validates); [c]null[/c] or any other non-digit character makes the result [c]false[/c].

member.name: ToBool
member.brief: Coerces a loosely-typed field to a bool, accepting the common yes/no spellings.

body.p: Use it to give a checkbox, text input, or query-string value definite boolean meaning inside a rule, rather than matching only the literal [c]"true"[/c]/[c]"false"[/c].

The value to coerce. [c]null[/c] becomes [c]false[/c]; an actual passes through; a string is matched case-insensitively - [c]"true"/"yes"/"1"/"on"[/c] give [c]true[/c] and [c]"false"/"no"/"0"/"off"[/c] give [c]false[/c]. Any other string, or any other type, is rejected with an exception, so use it only where the input is known to be one of these shapes.

member.name: ToInt
member.brief: Converts a possibly-textual field to an int for use in numeric rules.

body.p: Use it to bring a value that may arrive as text into integer arithmetic within a rule (e.g. comparing a typed-in quantity against a limit) without a separate parse step.

The value to convert. [c]null[/c] becomes [c]0[/c]; an actual passes through; a string is parsed as an integer and throws if it is not a valid one, so guard or pre-validate free text before relying on it. Other types are rejected.

member.name: IsNull
member.brief: Fluent, translatable stand-in for a null check.

body.p: Use it ([c]model.Field.IsNull()[/c]) when you want the null check to read as part of the member chain and to survive translation to JavaScript.
The value to test; the receiver of the extension call.

member.name: IsNullOrEmpty
member.brief: Fluent "required text" check - true when the string is missing or empty.

body.p: Use it where a form treats both a missing value and an empty string as "not provided", which is usually what you want.
The string to test. Both [c]null[/c] and [c]""[/c] yield [c]true[/c]; whitespace-only text does not.

member.name: IsNotNull
member.brief: Fluent, translatable not-null check.

body.p: Use it - the inverse of [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions.IsNull.T69]Functions.IsNull[/clink] - typically to guard a further check, e.g. [c]model.Child.IsNotNull() && model.Child.Age > 0[/c].
The value to test; the receiver of the extension call.

member.name: IsNotNullOrEmpty
member.brief: Fluent "text was supplied" check - true when the string is non-empty.

body.p: Use it - the inverse of [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.Functions.IsNullOrEmpty.yRG]Functions.IsNullOrEmpty[/clink] - to require a non-empty value.
The string to test. Returns [c]false[/c] for both [c]null[/c] and [c]""[/c].

class.name: IConstantTranslator
class.brief: Translates a constant value into a JavaScript literal.

member.name: TryTranslate
member.brief: Implement this to render constants of a type the built-ins don't handle.

body.p: Inspect , and return [c]false[/c] for values that aren't yours so another translator (or the built-ins) gets a turn.
The constant to render; test its type to decide whether to claim it.
Available for escaping strings and reading the date mode while building the literal.
Receives the JavaScript literal when you return [c]true[/c]; otherwise leave it null.

declaration.name: TryTranslate
declaration.params: object value, [link=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IExpressionEmitContext]Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IExpressionEmitContext[/link] context, out string js

class.name: IExpressionEmitContext
class.brief: The building blocks a method-call translator needs to emit JavaScript.

body.p: Implemented by [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler]ExpressionCompiler[/clink] ; delegates to its (overridable) walk/escape helpers so custom translators compose with subclass customizations such as parameter rendering.

member.name: DateMode
member.brief: The configured DateTime framing (Local or Utc).

member.name: Emit
member.brief: Emit the JavaScript for a sub-expression (walks the tree).

member.name: TryEvaluate
member.brief: Try to fold a (parameter-free) sub-expression to a constant value.

member.name: EmitPredicate
member.brief: Emit a lambda as a JavaScript function-expression predicate (function (...) { return ...; }).

member.name: EmitParameterAccess
member.brief: Emit access to a parameter-rooted member/indexer chain.

member.name: RootsInParameter
member.brief: True if the expression is (or roots in) one of the lambda's parameters.

member.name: EscapeString
member.brief: Render a CLR string as a JavaScript string literal.

member.name: EscapeRegex
member.brief: Escape a regex pattern for use inside a JavaScript /.../ literal.

class.name: IJsConstantRegistry
class.brief: Additive registry for custom constant types (Guid, enums, value structs, ...).

member.name: AddTranslator
member.brief: Register a fully custom constant translator.

body.p: Use this overload when rendering depends on more than the value's static type (e.g. emitting different literals for different values); for the common "this type always renders this way" case prefer [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsConstantRegistry.MapConstant.l14]IJsConstantRegistry.MapConstant[/clink] .
The translator to consult before the built-ins.

member.name: MapConstant
member.brief: Register a constant type by giving the function that renders it.

body.p: The concise way to add a type the built-ins don't cover (a , a domain value), or to override how one is emitted (e.g. an enum by name instead of by number).
The constant type this mapping applies to (and its subtypes).
Produces the JavaScript literal for a value of ; it owns any required escaping.

class.name: IJsMemberRegistry
class.brief: Additive registry for custom member/property access.

member.name: AddTranslator
member.brief: Register a fully custom member translator.

body.p: The translator to consult before the built-ins.

member.name: MapMember
member.brief: Map a property (by declaring type + name) to a JavaScript template.

body.p: The concise way to teach the compiler a property read it doesn't already know.
The type that declares the property to match.
The property name to match, exactly.
The JavaScript template; [c]$obj[/c] is replaced by the emitted target the property is read from.

declaration.name: MapMember
declaration.params: Type declaringType, string name, string template

class.name: IJsMethodRegistry
class.brief: Additive extension surface for mapping method calls to JavaScript.

body.p: Registrations are consulted [b]before[/b] the (fixed, correctly-ordered) built-ins, so a consumer can shadow a specific method without being able to reorder or remove built-ins - the built-in precedence invariants are preserved. Registrations are tried in the order added.

member.name: AddTranslator
member.brief: Register a custom translator (consulted before built-ins).

member.name: MapMethod
member.brief: Map a method (by type + name) to a JavaScript template ($obj, $0, $1, ...).

declaration.name: MapMethod
declaration.params: Type declaringType, string name, string template, int? argCount = null

member.name: MapMethod
member.brief: Map a specific method to a JavaScript template ($obj, $0, $1, ...).

class.name: IJsParameterRegistry
class.brief: Registry for binding a parameter type to a host-side lookup instead of emitting it verbatim.

body.p: By default a lambda parameter emits as itself - [c]m => m.Age[/c] becomes [c]m.Age[/c] - which assumes the client already has an object shaped like the parameter. For form validation you usually want a model parameter to resolve against the page instead, through a host-provided [c]reference('path')[/c] hook. Register the model type here (typically once, for the whole application) and every rule over that type emits against the form, with no subclassing.
Register before reading [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression.B2E]ExpressionCompiler.JavaScriptExpression[/clink] .

member.name: MapReference
member.brief: Bind the matching rule parameters to the built-in [c]reference()[/c] convention.

body.p: The bare parameter emits as [c]reference()[/c] and a member chain rooted in it emits as [c]reference('Path.To.Field')[/c] (array indexing becomes [c]jsv_index(...)[/c]) - the page supplies a [c]reference(path)[/c] function that returns the field's current value. This is the common case; use [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsParameterRegistry.Map.5EE]IJsParameterRegistry.Map[/clink] for any other shape.

The match runs on the parameter's type, but usually the exact type does not matter - a predicate such as [c]t => !t.IsPrimitive && t != typeof(string)[/c] (anything that looks like a model) is typical. Only the rule's own (top-level) parameters are matched; parameters introduced by a nested LINQ lambda always keep their own name.
Tells whether a rule parameter of the given type should bind to a reference.

member.name: Map
member.brief: Bind the matching rule parameters with custom rendering.

body.p: Use this when the host hook is not the default [c]reference()[/c] shape - for example a validated value bound to an ambient [c]value[/c], or a differently named lookup. As with
[clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IJsParameterRegistry.MapReference.lsF]IJsParameterRegistry.MapReference[/clink] , only the rule's own parameters are matched.
Tells whether a rule parameter of the given type should bind.
Renders the whole model - the bare parameter. The gives its type (and name) - for example [c]p => "value"[/c], or [c]p => "reference('" + p.Type.Name + "')"[/c].

Renders a field access, given the member/index chain and the root parameter it is rooted in. Use [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.ParameterAccessPath.HK7]ExpressionCompiler.ParameterAccessPath[/clink] to turn the chain into a dotted path - for example [c](e, p) => "value." + ExpressionCompiler.ParameterAccessPath(e)[/c] turns [c]m.Address.PostalCode[/c] into [c]value.Address.PostalCode[/c].

class.name: IMemberTranslator
class.brief: Translates a member/property access into JavaScript.

member.name: TryTranslate
member.brief: Implement this to emit a property/field read the built-ins don't cover.

body.p: Check the member's declaring type and name, and return [c]false[/c] for reads that aren't yours.
The member access to inspect; its [c]Member[/c] and [c]Expression[/c] tell you what is being read and from what.
Use it to emit the target expression ([c]member.Expression[/c]) and read the date mode.
Receives the emitted JavaScript when you return [c]true[/c]; otherwise leave it null.

declaration.name: TryTranslate
declaration.params: MemberExpression member, [link=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IExpressionEmitContext]Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IExpressionEmitContext[/link] context, out string js

class.name: IMethodCallTranslator
class.brief: Translates a specific family of method calls into JavaScript.

body.p: Translators are tried in order; the first whose [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IMethodCallTranslator.TryTranslate.g17]IMethodCallTranslator.TryTranslate[/clink] returns true wins. Register custom ones through [clink=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.ExpressionCompiler.Methods.p1B]ExpressionCompiler.Methods[/clink] ; registrations are consulted before the built-ins, so registering one is how you override a built-in translation.

member.name: TryTranslate
member.brief: Implement this to claim a method call and produce its JavaScript.

body.p: The compiler offers each call to the registered translators in turn, so return [c]false[/c] (and leave null) for calls you don't handle, and only consume the ones you recognize - keeping translators disjoint is what lets them be combined freely.
The call to inspect; check its method, declaring type, and arguments to decide whether it is yours.
Use it to emit sub-expressions, fold constants, escape strings, and read the date mode - so your output composes with the rest of the compiler.
Receives the emitted JavaScript when you return [c]true[/c]; otherwise leave it null.

declaration.name: TryTranslate
declaration.params: MethodCallExpression call, [link=Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IExpressionEmitContext]Gehtsoft.ExpressionToJs.IExpressionEmitContext[/link] context, out string js
